name: Queue
coreIntuition: A queue processes elements in FIFO order — first in, first out. While simple as a data structure, the queue's algorithmic power comes from BFS (layer-by-layer exploration) and from the **monotonic deque** — a double-ended queue that maintains elements in sorted order, enabling O(1) minimum/maximum over a sliding window. If you see 'sliding window minimum/maximum', think deque.
mentalModel: A queue is a line at a ticket counter — first person in line gets served first. A deque is a double-ended line where you can enter and exit from both ends. The monotonic deque is a clever twist: whenever a new person joins, everyone shorter (or less important) in front of them is removed, because they'll never be 'seen' — the new person will outlast them in the window.
whenToUse: You need BFS (Breadth-First Search) — the queue maintains exploration order
You need to process tasks in order of arrival (FIFO)
You need sliding window minimum/maximum (monotonic deque)
The problem involves scheduling or buffering with FIFO semantics
You need to implement a circular buffer or ring buffer

thinkingFramework.detail: If you need shortest path in unweighted graph or level-order traversal, use a queue for BFS. This is the most common use of queues in interviews.

thinkingFramework.step: 2. Is this a sliding window min/max problem?
thinkingFramework.detail: If you need the minimum or maximum in every window of size K, use a monotonic deque. Maintain elements in increasing order (for min) or decreasing order (for max). Pop from back when violated, pop from front when out of window.
Queues preserve insertion order. If you need FIFO processing (first task submitted gets processed first), use a queue. If priority matters more than order, use a heap instead.

commonTraps.trap: Using a list as a queue (O(n) dequeue from front)
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Use a proper deque (collections.deque in Python, ArrayDeque in Java). Popping from the front of a list/array is O(n) due to shifting.

commonTraps.trap: Confusing queue (FIFO) with stack (LIFO) in BFS/DFS
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Queue → BFS (layer by layer). Stack → DFS (depth first). Swapping them changes the algorithm entirely.

commonTraps.trap: Not removing expired elements from monotonic deque
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Store indices in the deque, not values. At each step, check if the front index is out of the current window and pop if so.

interviewVariantStrategy: Queue interview problems: (1) **BFS** — the most common queue application. (2) **Sliding window maximum** — monotonic deque. (3) **Design problems** — implement a queue using stacks, implement a circular queue, implement a recent counter. (4) **Task scheduling** — process tasks in FIFO order with constraints. The twist: 'sliding window maximum' sounds like a brute force problem but is O(n) with a monotonic deque. Recognizing this is the key interview insight for queues beyond BFS.

resources.title: NeetCode — Monotonic Deque Pattern
resources.why: Clear explanation of the monotonic deque technique for sliding window maximum/minimum

resources.title: VisuAlgo — Queue Visualization
resources.why: Interactive visualization of queue operations to understand FIFO behavior

resources.title: CP-Algorithms — Minimum Queue
resources.why: Shows how to augment a queue to support O(1) minimum/maximum queries
